questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Suppose the earth revolves around the sun in a circular orbit, maintaining a constant speed of $30 \text{ km/s}$.
options: From 1st Jan, 90 to 30th June, 90, the average velocity of the earth is zero.
During the aforementioned period, the average acceleration is $60 \text{ km/s}^2$.
From 1st Jan, 90 to 31st Dec, 90, the average speed of the earth is zero.
The instantaneous acceleration of the earth is directed towards the sun.
questions.solution:
In uniform circular motion, the instantaneous acceleration is the centripetal acceleration, which is always directed towards the centre of the circular path. Since the earth revolves around the sun in a circular orbit, its instantaneous acceleration is directed towards the sun.

From 1st Jan to 30th June (approximately half a year), the earth completes half of its orbit. The displacement is equal to the diameter of the orbit, which is non-zero, making the average velocity non-zero.

During this half-year period, the change in velocity is $2v = 60 \text{ km/s}$. The average acceleration is $\dfrac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{60}{\text{half a year in seconds}} \text{ km/s}^2$, which is much less than $60 \text{ km/s}^2$.

From 1st Jan to 31st Dec (one full year), the distance travelled is the circumference of the orbit. The average speed is the constant speed of $30 \text{ km/s}$, not zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car of mass $m$ attempts to navigate a circular road of radius $r$ which is banked for a speed $v = 40 \text{ km/hr}$. The coefficient of friction between the tyre and the road is negligible.
options: If the car attempts the turn at a speed lower than $40 \text{ km/hr}$, it will slip downwards
The car is unable to execute a turn without skidding
When the car turns at the exact speed of $40 \text{ km/hr}$, the force exerted by the road on the car exceeds $mg$ and also exceeds $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$
When the car turns at the exact speed of $40 \text{ km/hr}$, the force exerted by the road on the car equals $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$
questions.solution:
The road is banked at an angle $\theta$ such that $\tan \theta = \dfrac{v_0^2}{rg}$, where $v_0 = 40 \text{ km/hr}$.

When the car moves at a speed $v$, the forces acting on it are the normal force $N$ and gravity $mg$.

Resolving forces along the incline, the downward component of gravity is $mg \sin \theta$ and the required centripetal component is $\dfrac{mv^2}{r} \cos \theta$.

If $v < v_0$, $mg \sin \theta > \dfrac{mv^2}{r} \cos \theta$. Since friction is negligible, there is a net downward force along the incline, causing the car to slip downwards. Thus, option (1) is correct.

When the car moves at exactly $v = 40 \text{ km/hr}$, it does not slip. The normal force $N$ provides both the vertical balance and the centripetal force:

$N \cos \theta = mg$

$N \sin \theta = \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$

Squaring and adding these equations gives the total force exerted by the road on the car:

$N = \sqrt{(mg)^2 + \left(\dfrac{mv^2}{r}\right)^2}$

Since both $mg$ and $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$ are non-zero positive quantities, $N > mg$ and $N > \dfrac{mv^2}{r}$. Thus, option (3) is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A person exerts a constant force $\vec{F}$ on a particle of mass $m$ and observes that the particle travels in a circle of radius $r$ at a uniform speed $v$, as viewed from an inertial frame of reference.
options: This scenario is impossible
There are other forces acting on the particle
The resultant of the additional forces is $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$ directed towards the centre
The resultant of the additional forces varies in both magnitude and direction
questions.solution:
For a particle moving in a circle of radius $r$ with uniform speed $v$, the net force acting on it must have a constant magnitude of $\dfrac{mv^2}{r}$ and be directed towards the centre of the circular path.

Let this net force be $\vec{F}_{net}$. Since the position of the particle continuously changes, the direction of $\vec{F}_{net}$ also continuously changes. Thus, $\vec{F}_{net}$ is a variable force.

The person exerts a constant force $\vec{F}$. For the particle to experience the required net force $\vec{F}_{net}$, there must be additional forces acting on it. Let the resultant of these additional forces be $\vec{F}_{add}$.

We can write $\vec{F}_{net} = \vec{F} + \vec{F}_{add}$, which gives $\vec{F}_{add} = \vec{F}_{net} - \vec{F}$.

The magnitude of the additional force is given by $|\vec{F}_{add}|^2 = |\vec{F}_{net}|^2 + |\vec{F}|^2 - 2|\vec{F}_{net}||\vec{F}|\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{F}_{net}$ and $\vec{F}$.

As the particle moves along the circle, the direction of $\vec{F}_{net}$ rotates while $\vec{F}$ remains constant. Therefore, the angle $\theta$ continuously changes, causing the magnitude of $\vec{F}_{add}$ to vary. Its direction must also vary to ensure that the vector sum with the constant force $\vec{F}$ always points towards the centre.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle travels in a spiral path with constant speed, as shown in the figure.
options: The magnitude of the acceleration remains constant
The acceleration of the particle remains constant
The velocity of the particle remains constant
The magnitude of the acceleration decreases continuously
questions.solution:
For a particle moving with a constant speed $v$, the tangential acceleration is zero ($a_t = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$).

The only acceleration acting on the particle is the normal (centripetal) acceleration, which is responsible for changing the direction of motion. The magnitude of this acceleration is given by:
$a = a_n = \dfrac{v^2}{R}$
where $R$ is the radius of curvature of the path.

From the given figure, as the particle moves along the spiral path in the direction indicated by the arrow, it moves from the inner, tighter loops to the outer, wider loops. This means the radius of curvature $R$ of the path is continuously increasing.

Since the speed $v$ is constant and the radius of curvature $R$ is continuously increasing, the magnitude of the acceleration $a = \dfrac{v^2}{R}$ must decrease continuously.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object travels along a curved path. Which of the following quantities may remain constant during the motion?
options: magnitude of acceleration
acceleration
speed
velocity
questions.solution:
For an object moving along a curved path, the direction of motion changes continuously. Since velocity is a vector quantity, a change in direction implies that velocity cannot remain constant.

In uniform circular motion, the object moves along a circular path with a constant speed. The magnitude of its acceleration, given by $\dfrac{v^2}{r}$, also remains constant.

In projectile motion, the object follows a parabolic curved path under the influence of gravity. Throughout the motion, the acceleration is constant in both magnitude and direction ($\vec{g}$ downwards).

Therefore, the speed, acceleration, and magnitude of acceleration may remain constant during motion along a curved path.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car of mass $M$ travels on a horizontal circular path of radius $r$. At a given instant, its speed is $v$ and is increasing at a rate $a$.
options: The acceleration of the car is directed towards the centre of the path
The magnitude of the frictional force acting on the car is greater than $\dfrac{Mv^2}{r}$
The coefficient of friction between the ground and the car is $\mu = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{v^2}{rg}\right)$
The coefficient of friction between the ground and the car is not less than $a/g$
questions.solution:
The car moving on a horizontal circular path with an increasing speed has two components of acceleration:

Tangential acceleration, $a_t = a$

Centripetal acceleration, $a_c = \dfrac{v^2}{r}$

The net acceleration of the car is given by $a_{net} = \sqrt{a_t^2 + a_c^2} = \sqrt{a^2 + \left(\dfrac{v^2}{r}\right)^2}$.

Since there is a tangential component, the net acceleration is not directed towards the centre of the path.

The required force is provided by the frictional force $f$ between the car and the ground. Thus, $f = M a_{net} = M \sqrt{a^2 + \left(\dfrac{v^2}{r}\right)^2}$.

Since $a > 0$, the magnitude of the frictional force is strictly greater than $\dfrac{Mv^2}{r}$.

For the car to move without slipping, the required frictional force must be less than or equal to the limiting friction, $f \le \mu Mg$.

$\mu Mg \ge M \sqrt{a^2 + \left(\dfrac{v^2}{r}\right)^2}$

$\mu \ge \dfrac{1}{g} \sqrt{a^2 + \left(\dfrac{v^2}{r}\right)^2}$

Since $\sqrt{a^2 + \left(\dfrac{v^2}{r}\right)^2} > a$, it follows that $\mu > \dfrac{a}{g}$.

Therefore, the coefficient of friction is not less than $\dfrac{a}{g}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During circular motion about the origin, a particle's position vector sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time. Its
options: velocity stays constant
tangential acceleration stays constant
acceleration stays constant
speed stays constant
questions.solution:
The areal velocity of a particle is given by $\dfrac{dA}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{2} r^2 \omega$.

Since the position vector sweeps out equal areas in equal intervals of time, the areal velocity is constant.

For circular motion about the origin, the radius $r$ is constant. Therefore, the angular velocity $\omega$ must also be constant.

The speed of the particle is $v = r\omega$. Since both $r$ and $\omega$ are constant, the speed $v$ remains constant.

Because the speed is constant, the tangential acceleration is $a_t = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = 0$. Since it is zero, it remains constant.

The direction of motion changes continuously, meaning the velocity vector is not constant.

The total acceleration is purely centripetal ($a_c = r\omega^2$) and its direction continuously changes as it always points towards the origin. Thus, the acceleration vector is not constant.
